SUMMARY:
The branches of science known informally as _omics_ are various disciplines in biology whose names end in the suffix _-omics_, such as genomics, proteomics, metabolomics, metagenomics, phenomics and transcriptomics. The related suffix **-ome** is used to address the objects of study of such fields, such as the genome, proteome or metabolome respectively. *   Nucleomics: Study of the complete set of genomic components which form "the cell nucleus as a complex, dynamic biological system, referred to as the nucleome". ^ _**a**__**b**__**c**__**d**__**e**__**f**__**g**__**h**_González, Adriana; Fullaondo, Asier; Odriozola, Adrián (2024-01-01), Martínez, Adrián Odriozola (ed.), "Chapter Two - Techniques, procedures, and applications in microbiome analysis", _Advances in Genetics_, Advances in Host Genetics and microbiome in lifestyle-related phenotypes, **111**, Academic Press: 81–115, doi "Doi (identifier)"):10.1016/bs.adgen.2024.01.003, PMID "PMID (identifier)")38908906, retrieved 2024-11-22

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Suffix in biology

For the journal, see [OMICS (journal)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OMICS_(journal) "OMICS (journal)"). For the predatory journal publisher, see [OMICS Publishing Group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OMICS_Publishing_Group "OMICS Publishing Group").

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Genome-en.svg/500px-Genome-en.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Genome-en.svg)

Diagram illustrating genomics

**Omics** is the collective characterization and quantification of entire sets of [biological molecules](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biological_molecule "Biological molecule") and the investigation of how they translate into the structure, function, and dynamics of an [organism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organism "Organism") or group of organisms.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-Yamada2021-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-2) The branches of [science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science "Science") known informally as _omics_ are various disciplines in [biology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biology "Biology") whose names end in the suffix _[-omics](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-omics "wikt:-omics")_, such as [genomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genomics "Genomics"), [proteomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proteomics "Proteomics"), [metabolomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metabolomics "Metabolomics"), [metagenomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metagenomics "Metagenomics"), [phenomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomics "Phenomics") and [transcriptomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transcriptomics "Transcriptomics").

The related suffix **-ome** is used to address the objects of study of such fields, such as the [genome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genome "Genome"), [proteome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proteome "Proteome") or [metabolome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metabolome "Metabolome") respectively. The suffix _-ome_ as used in molecular biology refers to a _totality_ of some sort; it is an example of a "neo-suffix" formed by abstraction from various Greek terms in -ωμα, a sequence that does not form an identifiable suffix in Greek.

[Functional genomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Functional_genomics "Functional genomics") aims at identifying the functions of as many genes as possible of a given organism. It combines different -omics techniques such as transcriptomics and proteomics with saturated mutant collections.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-3)

Origin
------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Omics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origin")]

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Omicum.jpg/250px-Omicum.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Omicum.jpg)

"Omicum": Building of the [Estonian Biocentre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Estonian_Biocentre "Estonian Biocentre") which houses the [Estonian Genome Centre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Estonian_Genome_Project "Estonian Genome Project") and Institute of Molecular and Cell Biology at the [University of Tartu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Tartu "University of Tartu") in Tartu, [Estonia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Estonia "Estonia").

The _[Oxford English Dictionary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxford\_English\_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")_ (_OED_) distinguishes three different fields of application for the _-ome_ suffix:

1.   in medicine, forming nouns with the sense "swelling, tumour"
2.   in botany or zoology, forming nouns in the sense "a part of an animal or plant with a specified structure"
3.   in cellular and molecular biology, forming nouns with the sense "all constituents considered collectively"

The _-ome_ suffix originated as a variant of _-oma_, and became productive in the last quarter of the 19th century. It originally appeared in terms like _[sclerome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sclerome "Sclerome")_[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-scleroma-4) or _[rhizome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rhizome "Rhizome")_.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-rhizome-5) All of these terms derive from Greek words in -ωμα,[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-oma-6) a sequence that is not a single suffix, but analyzable as -ω-μα, the -ω- belonging to the word stem (usually a verb) and the -μα being a genuine Greek suffix forming abstract nouns.

The OED suggests that its third definition originated as a [back-formation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Back-formation "Back-formation") from _[mitome](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mitome&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mitome (page does not exist)")_,[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-urlHome_:_Oxford_English_Dictionary-7) Early attestations include _[biome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biome "Biome")_ (1916)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-urlbiome,_n._:_Oxford_English_Dictionary-8) and _[genome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genome "Genome")_ (first coined as German _Genom_ in 1920[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-genom-9)).[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-omic_dict-10)

The association with _chromosome_ in molecular biology is by [false etymology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Folk_etymology "Folk etymology"). The word _chromosome_ derives from the [Greek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language")[stems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Word_stem "Word stem")χρωμ(ατ)- "colour" and σωμ(ατ)- "body".[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-omic_dict-10) While σωμα "body" genuinely contains the -μα suffix, the preceding -ω- is not a stem-forming suffix but part of the word's [root](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Root_(linguistics) "Root (linguistics)"). Because _genome_ refers to the _complete_ genetic makeup of an organism, a neo-suffix _-ome_ suggested itself as referring to "wholeness" or "completion".[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-liddell-11)

[Bioinformaticians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bioinformatics "Bioinformatics") and [molecular biologists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecular_biologist "Molecular biologist") figured amongst the first scientists to apply the "-ome" suffix widely.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_] Early advocates included bioinformaticians in [Cambridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge"), UK, where there were many early bioinformatics labs such as the [MRC centre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MRC_centre "MRC centre"), [Sanger centre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanger_centre "Sanger centre"), and EBI ([European Bioinformatics Institute](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Bioinformatics_Institute "European Bioinformatics Institute")); for example, the MRC centre carried out the [first genome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whole_genome_sequencing "Whole genome sequencing") and proteome projects.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-12)

Current usage
-------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Omics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Current usage")]

Many "omes" beyond the original "[genome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genome "Genome")" have become useful and have been widely adopted by research scientists. "[Proteomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proteomics "Proteomics")" has become well-established as a term for studying [proteins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proteins "Proteins") at a large scale. "Omes" can provide an easy shorthand to encapsulate a field; for example, an [interactomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interactomics "Interactomics") study is clearly recognisable as relating to large-scale analyses of gene-gene, protein-protein, or protein-ligand interactions. Researchers are rapidly taking up omes and omics, as shown by the explosion of the use of these terms in [PubMed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PubMed "PubMed") since the mid-1990s.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-13)

### Genomics

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Omics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Genomics")]

*   [Genomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genomics "Genomics"): Study of the [genomes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genome "Genome") of organisms. 
    *   [Cognitive genomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cognitive_genomics "Cognitive genomics"): Study of the changes in [cognitive processes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cognitive_process "Cognitive process") associated with genetic profiles.
    *   [Comparative genomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparative_genomics "Comparative genomics"): Study of the relationship of genome structure and function across different biological species or strains.
    *   [Functional genomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Functional_genomics "Functional genomics"): Describes gene and protein functions and interactions (often uses transcriptomics).
    *   [Metagenomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metagenomics "Metagenomics"): Study of [metagenomes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metagenome "Metagenome"), i.e., genetic material recovered directly from environmental samples.
    *   [Neurogenomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neurogenomics "Neurogenomics"): Study of genetic influences on the development and function of the nervous system.
    *   [Pangenomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pangenomics "Pangenomics"): Study of the entire collection of genes or genomes found within a given species.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-14)
    *   [Personal genomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Personal_genomics "Personal genomics"): Branch of genomics concerned with the sequencing and analysis of the genome of an individual. Once the genotypes are known, the individual's genotype can be compared with the published literature to determine likelihood of trait expression and disease risk. Helps in Personalized Medicine
    *   Electromics: Branch of genomics concerned with the role of exogenous electric fields in potentiating the gene expression profiles of cells, tissues, and organoids.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-15)

### Epigenomics

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Omics&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Epigenomics")]

The [epigenome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenome "Epigenome") is the supporting structure of the genome, including protein and RNA binders, alternative DNA structures, and chemical modifications on DNA.

*   [Epigenomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenomics "Epigenomics"): Modern technologies include chromosome conformation by [Hi-C](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chromosome_conformation_capture "Chromosome conformation capture"), various [ChIP-seq](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ChIP-seq "ChIP-seq") and other sequencing methods combined with proteomic fractionations, and sequencing methods that find chemical modification of cytosines, like bisulfite sequencing.
*   Nucleomics: Study of the complete set of genomic components which form "the cell nucleus as a complex, dynamic biological system, referred to as the nucleome".[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-16)[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-17) The 4D Nucleome Consortium officially joined the IHEC ([International Human Epigenome Consortium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Human_Epigenome_Consortium "International Human Epigenome Consortium")) in 2017.

### Microbiomics

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Omics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Microbiomics")]

The _[microbiome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microbiome "Microbiome")_ is a microbial community occupying a well-defined habitat with distinct physio-chemical properties. It includes the microorganisms involved and their theatre of activity, forming [ecological niches.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecological_niche "Ecological niche") Microbiomes form dynamic and interactive micro-ecosystems prone to spaciotemporal change. They are integrated into macro-ecosystems, such as eukaryotic hosts, and are crucial to the host's proper function and health.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-Berg2020-18) The interactive host-microbe systems make up the [holobiont](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holobiont "Holobiont").[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-:0-19)

Microbiomics is the study of microbiome dynamics, function, and structure.[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-20) This area of study employs several techniques to study the microbiome in its host environment:[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-:0-19)

*   Sampling methods focused on collecting representative samples of the local environment, either from oral swabs or stool.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-:0-19)
*   [Culturomics (microbiology)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Culturomics_(microbiology) "Culturomics (microbiology)") is the [high-throughput](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High_throughput_biology "High throughput biology")[cell culture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_culture "Cell culture") of [bacteria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") that aims to comprehensively identify [strains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bacterial_strain "Bacterial strain") or species in samples obtained from tissues such as the [human gut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human_digestive_system "Human digestive system") or from the [environment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_environment "Natural environment").[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-Lagier2012-21)[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-GG-22)
*   [Microfluidics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microfluidics "Microfluidics")[gut-on-a-chip](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organ-on-a-chip "Organ-on-a-chip") devices, which simulate the conditions of the gut and allow analysis of changes to the microbiome that can be more accurately monitored than _in situ[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite\_note-:0-19)_.
*   Mechanical DNA extraction techniques and gene amplification methods, such as [PCR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polymerase_chain_reaction "Polymerase chain reaction"), to analyze the genomic profile of the entire microbiome.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-:0-19)
*   [DNA fingerprinting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DNA_profiling "DNA profiling") using microarrays and hybridization techniques allow analysis of shifts in microbiota populations.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-:0-19)
*   Multi-omics studies allow for functional analysis of microbiota.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-:0-19)
*   [Animal models](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Model_organism "Model organism") can be used to take more accurate samples of the _in situ_ microbiome. [Germ-free animals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Germ-free_animal "Germ-free animal") are used to implant a specific microbiome from another organism to yield a gnotobiotic model. These can be studied to see how it changes under different environmental conditions.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-:0-19)

### Lipidomics

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Omics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Lipidomics")]

The [lipidome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lipidome "Lipidome") is the entire complement of cellular [lipids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lipid "Lipid"), including the modifications made to a particular set of lipids, produced by an organism or system.

*   [Lipidomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lipidomics "Lipidomics"): Large-scale study of pathways and networks of lipids. Mass spectrometry techniques are used.

### Proteomics

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Omics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Proteomics")]

The [proteome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proteome "Proteome") is the entire complement of [proteins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein "Protein"), including the modifications made to a particular set of proteins, produced by an organism or system.

*   [Proteomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proteomics "Proteomics"): Large-scale study of proteins, particularly their structures and functions. Mass spectrometry techniques are used. 
    *   [Chemoproteomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemoproteomics "Chemoproteomics"): An array of techniques used to study protein-small molecule interactions
    *   [Immunoproteomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immunoproteomics "Immunoproteomics"): Study of large sets of proteins (proteomics) involved in the immune response
    *   Nutriproteomics: Identifying the molecular targets of nutritive and non-nutritive components of the diet. Uses proteomics mass spectrometry data for protein expression studies
    *   [Proteogenomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proteogenomics "Proteogenomics"): An emerging field of biological research at the intersection of proteomics and genomics. Proteomics data used for gene annotations.
    *   [Structural genomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Structural_genomics "Structural genomics"): Study of the three-dimensional structure of every protein encoded by a given genome using a combination of experimental and modeling approaches.

### Glycomics

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Omics&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Glycomics")]

[Glycomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycomics "Glycomics") is the comprehensive study of the [glycome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycome "Glycome") i.e. sugars and carbohydrates.

### Foodomics

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Omics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Foodomics")]

[Foodomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foodomics "Foodomics") was defined by [Alejandro Cifuentes](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Alejandro_Cifuentes&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alejandro Cifuentes (page does not exist)") in 2009 as "a discipline that studies the food and nutrition domains through the application and integration of advanced omics technologies to improve consumer’s well-being, health, and knowledge."[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-Gunn-23)[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omics#cite_note-Cifuentes-24)

### Transcriptomics

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Omics&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Transcriptomics")]

[Transcriptome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transcriptome "Transcriptome") is the set of all [RNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RNA "RNA") molecules, including mRNA, rRNA, tRNA, and other non-coding RNA, produced in one or a population of cells.

*   [Transcriptomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transcriptomics "Transcriptomics"): Study of transcriptomes, their structures and functions.

### Metabolomics

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Omics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Metabolomics")]

The [metabolome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metabolome "Metabolome") is the ensemble of small molecules found within a biological matrix.

*   [Metabolomics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metabolomics "Metabolomics"): Scientific study of chemical processes involving metabolites. It is a "systematic study of the unique chemical fingerprints that specific cellular processes leave behind", the study of their small-molecule metabolite profiles

## **Omics Technologies**

Omics technologies refer to methods used to analyse biological molecules on a large scale, providing comprehensive insights into the structure, function, and dynamics of organisms. Omics technologies include:

**Bulk Omics**: Large-scale analyses of a pool of cells.

**Single-Cell Omics:** Focuses on the analysis of individual cells.

**Spatial Omics**: Examines the spatial organisation and interactions of molecules within tissues.

Abstract
--------

The human history has witnessed the rapid development of technologies such as high-throughput sequencing and mass spectrometry that led to the concept of “omics” and methodological advancement in systematically interrogating a cellular system. Yet, the ever-growing types of molecules and regulatory mechanisms being discovered have been persistently transforming our understandings on the cellular machinery. This renders cell omics seemingly, like the universe, expand with no limit and our goal toward the complete harness of the cellular system merely impossible. Therefore, it is imperative to review what has been done and is being done to predict what can be done toward the translation of omics information to disease control with minimal cell perturbation. With a focus on the “four big omics,” i.e., genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, we delineate hierarchies of these omics together with their epiomics and interactomics, and review technologies developed for interrogation. We predict, among others, redoxomics as an emerging omics layer that views cell decision toward the physiological or pathological state as a fine-tuned redox balance.

**Keywords:** omics, next generation sequencing, third generation sequencing, mass spectrometry, redoxomics

Introduction
------------

“OMICS,” defined as probing and analyzing large amount of data representing the structure and function of an entire makeup of a given biological system at a particular level, has substantially revolutionized our methodologies in interrogating biological systems. In other words, “top down” approaches, largely attributable to “omics” development, coupled with “bottom up” strategies to offer a holistic tool for efficient biological system investigation. The concept of dissecting complex disorders including cancers has been, accordingly, advanced from static delineation between cell malignant and heathy states in a low-throughput manner to spatio-temporal dynamic deconvolution of complex systems involving multi-layer modifications at genomic, transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolic levels in a global-unbiased fashion.

Ever since the establishment of the first high-throughput technology, DNA microarray ([1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9289742/#B1)), technologies for omics exploration have been developed by leaps and bounds. Following the central dogma, omics technologies have been used to capture the static genomic alterations, temporal transcriptomic perturbations and alternative splicing, as well as spatio-temporal proteomic dynamics and post translational modifications (PTMs) ([2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9289742/#B2)). Beyond this, omics technologies have been expanded to analyze various omics at the epi-level (such as epigenome, epitranscriptome, epiproteome that are defined as the collection of all modifications of the referred omics beyond information it covered in a single cell), molecular interactions (i.e., varied levels of interactome), and disease associated hallmarks as metabolome and immunome. Multi-omics integration has become a prevailing trend for constructing a comprehensive causal relationship between molecular signatures and phenotypic manifestations of a particular disease, and single cell sequencing offers additional resolving power that enables investigations at a single cell level. This rapidly-developing and ever-growing field, omics, has empowered us to uncover the intricate molecular mechanism underlying different phenotypic manifestations of disordered traits in an overwhelming and systematic manner at a high accuracy. However, the complexity of the cellular behavior and its decision-making system may persistently drive the establishment of novel omics and associated techniques.

While we are running close to the truth in principle, the ever-growing knowledge on cellular omics persistently transforms our understandings toward cell machinery complexity that challenges our goal toward the fully harness of cell pathological state rewiring. It is, thus, time to comprehensively review what has been done and is being done in omics-relevant studies to forecast what can be done in “omics” as a shortcut toward our goal. Focusing on the four big omics, i.e., genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics and metabolics, their epiomics and pair-wise interactomics, this paper comprehensively reviews high-throughput technologies developed, and forecasts, among others, the emerging role of “redoxomics” on the cell machinery.

Technology-Based Omics
----------------------

Sequencing and mass spectrometry (MS) are basic experimental tools availing in our tour in investigating the omics of a given biological system. While sequencing-based approaches are feasible for studies on genome, transcriptome, their epitomes and interactomes involving DNA/RNA, MS-based techniques can be used to interrogate proteome, metabolome, and interactomes that do not involve DNA/RNA ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9289742/#F1), [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9289742/#T1)).

Conceptual illustration on the hierarchy of different omics covered in this paper. We classify omics technologies into two categories, i.e., technology- and knowledge- based. Technology-based omics are based on technologies developed for understanding the “central dogma,” which can be further divided into three groups, i.e., the “four big omics” (genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics), epiomics (epigenomics, epitranscriptomics, and epiproteomics), and their interactomics (DNA-RNA interactomics, RNA-RNA interactomics, DNA-protein interactomics, RNA-protein interactomics, protein-protein interactomics, and protein-metabolite interactomics). Omics indicated by the horizontal (above) and vertical (right-hand side) pink boxes of each interactomic term constitute to its two interacting omics. Knowledge-based omics are developed to understand a particular knowledge domain in a systematic way through integrating multiple omics information. Examples of this category include immunomics, microbiomics, and beyond.

# What is Omics?

Explore the branch of research known as “**omics**“–including genomics, transcriptomics, and proteomics.

![Flow chart of central dogma. A DNA molecule with an arrow to an RNA molecule with a label of "transcription," and then an arrow from the RNA molecule to a protein molecule with a label of "translation." Under DNA, there is a text box that reads "Genomics: the study of all genes found within a cell." Under RNA, there is a text box that reads "Transcriptomics: the study of all RNA found within a cell." Under protein, there is a text box that reads: "Proteomics: the study of all proteins found within a cell."](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20753%20337'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Flow chart of central dogma. A DNA molecule with an arrow to an RNA molecule with a label of "transcription," and then an arrow from the RNA molecule to a protein molecule with a label of "translation." Under DNA, there is a text box that reads "Genomics: the study of all genes found within a cell." Under RNA, there is a text box that reads "Transcriptomics: the study of all RNA found within a cell." Under protein, there is a text box that reads: "Proteomics: the study of all proteins found within a cell."](https://alleninstitute.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/What-is-proteomics-6.png)

**“Omics”** refers to a branch of research in the biological sciences focused on studying **all** of a particular type of molecule found within a living system.

This research includes techniques that share the suffix of **-omics**, such as: 

## Types of Omics

![Double-stranded DNA with a region highlighted and titled "gene A," an unlabled region of DNA in the center, and another highlighted region titled "gene B."](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20490%20102'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Double-stranded DNA with a region highlighted and titled "gene A," an unlabled region of DNA in the center, and another highlighted region titled "gene B."](https://alleninstitute.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/What-is-proteomics-11.png)

While **genetics** is the study of individual genes, **genomics** is the study of **all of the genes** found within a particular cell or organism ([National Human Genome Research Institute](https://www.genome.gov/about-genomics/fact-sheets/Genetics-vs-Genomics)). By studying all of the genes within a genome, we can gain a better understanding of the sequence of each of these genes, their relative locations within the genome, and what they code for within the cell.  

Perhaps the most famous example of genomics research is the [Human Genome Project](https://www.genome.gov/human-genome-project) where scientists successfully sequenced the entire human genome for the first time. Understanding the full sequence of a genome tells us valuable information about which genes a cell *could* express. However, not all genes in a cell’s genome are expressed all of the time. Instead, most genes are turned “on” and “off” at different points in a cell’s life cycle. Gene expression can not only vary across time but can also differ between different types of cells within the body. For example, the cells that make up your heart express different genes than the cells that make up your skin.  

While genomics allows us to study which genes a cell *could* express, we have to rely on other methods, such as **transcriptomics** or **proteomics**, to determine which genes a cell is *actually expressing* at a given point in time. 

![A long DNA molecule with three sections each with different colors. The first section is labeled "Gene A" with an arrow pointing down to 4 RNA molecules, labeled "RNA transcribed from Gene A." The last section of DNA is labeled "Gene B" with an arrow pointing down to 2 RNA molecules, labeled "RNA transcribed from Gene B."](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20531%20372'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![A long DNA molecule with three sections each with different colors. The first section is labeled "Gene A" with an arrow pointing down to 4 RNA molecules, labeled "RNA transcribed from Gene A." The last section of DNA is labeled "Gene B" with an arrow pointing down to 2 RNA molecules, labeled "RNA transcribed from Gene B."](https://alleninstitute.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/What-is-proteomics-7.png)

**Transcriptomics** is the study of all of the **RNA transcripts** found within a cell at a given point in time. This includes measuring *coding RNA*, which is RNA that will be translated into a protein, and *non-coding RNA*, which carries out a separate function without necessarily being translated into a protein.  

 By sequencing all of the RNA found within a cell, we can work “backwards” and determine which gene each molecule of RNA was transcribed from. Transcriptomics not only evaluates which RNA molecules are present/absent in a cell but also the quantity of the RNA molecules.  

For example, if we are measuring RNA transcripts within a hypothetical cell, we might find twice the amount of RNA transcripts from gene A compared to the RNA transcripts from gene B. This information allows us to infer that at the point in time when we measured the RNA within this cell, the cell was expressing gene A twice as much as gene B. If the functions of gene A and gene B are known, we can then use these data on their relative expression to infer what internal processes were taking place at the time when the cell was sampled.   

At the Allen Institute, we use transcriptomics for multiple lines of research—including our efforts to [map the human brain](https://alleninstitute.org/news/global-effort-to-map-the-human-brain-releases-first-data/) and [map the healthy human immune system](https://alleninstitute.org/news/allen-institute-for-immunology-unveils-landmark-human-immune-health-atlas/).  

![A diagram showing the central dogma. Gene A is being expressed at higher levels than Gene B as evidenced by the fact that there are twice as many protein A molecules as protein B molecules present.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20586%20564'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![A diagram showing the central dogma. Gene A is being expressed at higher levels than Gene B as evidenced by the fact that there are twice as many protein A molecules as protein B molecules present.](https://alleninstitute.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/What-is-proteomics-12.png)

**Proteomics** refers to the study of all of the proteins within a given cell and/or organism. A [**proteome**](https://www.cancer.gov/publications/dictionaries/cancer-terms/def/proteome) refers to the **entire list of proteins** made by a cell.

Proteins carry out the majority of functions and activities that take place within a living system. For example, **insulin**, which helps regulate our blood sugar levels, **hemoglobin**, which helps carry oxygen within our blood, and **antibodies**, which help the immune system fight foreign pathogens, are all examples of proteins that perform vital functions for our health and wellbeing.  

Because proteins are responsible for so many vital functions within a given cell, system, and/or organism, studying **proteomics** helps us gain insight into the processes, mechanisms, and/or pathways that are critical to health and disease.  

Similarly to transcriptomics, proteomics not only evaluates *which* proteins are present/absent in a living system at a given point in time, but also *how much* of these proteins are present. If we measure proteins within a hypothetical cell, we might find twice as much of protein A compared to protein B. These data tell us that gene A, which is transcribed and then translated into protein A, is being expressed roughly twice as much as gene B.  

Knowing which proteins are more or less abundant within a cell and/or organism can be extremely helpful when studying health and disease. For example, our scientists have used proteomics in multiple lines of research, including our efforts [to better understand long COVID](https://alleninstitute.org/news/many-long-covid-patients-suffer-from-persistent-inflammation-study-finds/).  

Why Omics?

Performing Omics research has become increasingly popular in recent years due to its ability to provide a more in-depth, comprehensive look into biological systems. For example, by using proteomics to study all of the proteins within a cell rather than just a select few, scientists can gain a more holistic picture into the structure and function of individual cells.  

In addition to individual Omics projects like genomics, transcriptomics, or proteomics, **multi-omic profiling** has also emerged as an increasingly popular approach. This approach refers to the practice of collecting data for multiple Omics simultaneously—such as studying both the transcriptomic and proteomic profile of a given cell or system instead of just one or the other.  

Here, we overviewed three types of Omics research. However, the field of Omics includes a wide variety of other methods to gain a holistic picture of different molecules within a cell, including approaches like [epigenomics](https://www.genome.gov/about-genomics/fact-sheets/Epigenomics-Fact-Sheet) and [metabolomics](https://dceg.cancer.gov/research/how-we-study/metabolomics).

URL: https://www.britannica.com/science/omics

SUMMARY:
**genomics**, one of several omic branches of biological study, concentrates on the structure, function, and inheritance of an organism’s genome (its entire set of genetic material) . The genomic DNA sequence is contained within an organism’s chromosomes, one or more sets of which are found in each cell of an organism. Often a gene identified by forward genetics has been mapped to one specific chromosomal region, and the full genomic sequence reveals a gene in this position with an already annotated function.

**omics**, any of several areas of biological study defined by the investigation of the entire complement of a specific type of [biomolecule](https://www.britannica.com/science/biomolecule) or the totality of a molecular process within an organism. In [biology](https://www.britannica.com/science/biology) the word _omics_ refers to the sum of [constituents](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/constituents) within a [cell](https://www.britannica.com/science/cell-biology). The omics sciences share the overarching aim of identifying, describing, and quantifying the biomolecules and molecular processes that contribute to the form and function of cells and [tissues](https://www.britannica.com/science/tissue).

Branches
--------

There are many branches of omics sciences. Examples of well-established fields include [genomics](https://www.britannica.com/science/genomics), proteomics, and [metabolomics](https://www.britannica.com/science/metabolomics). Genomics considers the totality of the structure and function of an organism’s genome (the entire set of [genes](https://www.britannica.com/science/gene)), while proteomics focuses on the proteome—the entire collection of [proteins](https://www.britannica.com/science/protein) in an organism’s cells. Metabolomics is concerned with [metabolism](https://www.britannica.com/science/metabolism) and, more specifically, with the function and interactions of metabolic breakdown products, or metabolites. Other branches of omics include transcriptomics, the study of the full complement of [RNA](https://www.britannica.com/science/RNA) in an organism’s cells, and lipidomics, which focuses on [lipids](https://www.britannica.com/science/lipid) and pathways involved in [lipid](https://www.britannica.com/science/lipid) signaling. The interdisciplinary nature of the omics sciences is reflected particularly in the field of interactomics, which brings together biology and [bioinformatics](https://www.britannica.com/science/bioinformatics) to investigate the relationships and interplay between proteins and other molecules and the significance of those interactions.

Technologies and data management
--------------------------------

Technologies that are paramount to omics sciences include various high-throughput (automated) biochemical assays and software and databases that allow for complex [integrated](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/integrated) analyses. In genomics, for example, next-generation sequencing and assays designed to detect genetic variations play an essential role in identifying and characterizing genes. [Microarray](https://www.britannica.com/technology/microarray) analysis and RNA sequencing likewise serve a critical role in transcriptomics, while microarray, liquid [chromatography](https://www.britannica.com/science/chromatography), and [mass spectrometry](https://www.britannica.com/science/mass-spectrometry) are examples of technologies used for the identification and characterization of proteins.

[![Image 6: greylag. Flock of Greylag geese during their winter migration at Bosque del Apache National Refugee, New Mexico. greylag goose (Anser anser)](https://cdn.britannica.com/12/167112-131-ABF5C65B/greylag-Flock-Greylag-geese-winter-migration-goose.jpg) Britannica Quiz Biology Bonanza](https://www.britannica.com/quiz/biology-bonanza)
Such high-throughput assays generate large amounts of data, which can be used for modeling and other forms of analysis but which also present challenges for data storage. Multiple databases have been constructed to house data from omics studies and to render the data publicly available. Examples include the Cancer Genome Atlas, the International Cancer Genome [Consortium](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Consortium), the ProteomeXchange Consortium, the Proteomics Identifications Database, and the Human Metabolome Database.

Impact and challenges
---------------------

The impact of omics is most apparent in [medicine](https://www.britannica.com/science/medicine). Sequencing of the [human genome](https://www.britannica.com/science/human-genome), for example, has fueled advances in [personalized medicine](https://www.britannica.com/science/personalized-medicine), in which decisions about disease prevention,[diagnosis](https://www.britannica.com/science/diagnosis), and treatment are tailored to patients based on information derived from genetic and genomic research. In particular, genomic data have played key roles in the development of predictive models of disease and in informing therapeutic decisions, such as in the treatment of [cancer](https://www.britannica.com/science/cancer-disease). Similar links between omics and personalized medicine have emerged from metabolomics with the discovery of new [biomarkers](https://www.britannica.com/science/biomarker) of disease. An example is the investigation of disturbances in metabolic pathways that affect levels of substances such as [fatty acids](https://www.britannica.com/science/fatty-acid) and [bile](https://www.britannica.com/science/bile) acids; this work has led to the identification of biomarkers with the potential to improve early [diagnosis](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/diagnosis) of hepatocellular carcinoma.

Nonetheless, significant challenges remain in the omics sciences, especially concerning data complexity, data management, and the [integration](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/integration) of data from omics studies with data from other sources, such as clinical data gathered during routine physician visits. Other challenges are more fundamental, such as in assay development and refinement. In large-scale proteomic analysis, for instance, agents designed to bind to specific proteins often are lacking in [sensitivity and specificity](https://www.britannica.com/science/sensitivity-medical-statistics), decreasing their [affinity](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/affinity) for the proteins of interest and resulting in suboptimal [protein](https://www.britannica.com/science/protein) capture.

**genomics**, one of several [omic](https://www.britannica.com/science/omics) branches of biological study, concentrates on the structure, function, and [inheritance](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/inheritance) of an organism’s [genome](https://www.britannica.com/science/genome) (its entire set of genetic material) . heredityprotein

A major part of genomics is determining the sequence of molecules that make up the genomic deoxyribonucleic acid ([DNA](https://www.britannica.com/science/DNA)) content of an organism. The genomic DNA sequence is contained within an organism’s [chromosomes](https://www.britannica.com/science/chromosome), one or more sets of which are found in each cell of an organism. The chromosomes can be further described as containing the fundamental units of [heredity](https://www.britannica.com/science/heredity-genetics), the [genes](https://www.britannica.com/science/gene). Genes are transcriptional units, those regions of chromosomes that under appropriate circumstances are capable of producing a ribonucleic acid ([RNA](https://www.britannica.com/science/RNA)) transcript that can be translated into molecules of [protein](https://www.britannica.com/science/protein).

[![Image 22: meiosis](https://cdn.britannica.com/75/94975-004-D0A70DCA/event-meiosis-crossing-combinations-recombination-part-region.jpg?w=300)](https://cdn.britannica.com/75/94975-004-D0A70DCA/event-meiosis-crossing-combinations-recombination-part-region.jpg)

[meiosis](https://cdn.britannica.com/75/94975-004-D0A70DCA/event-meiosis-crossing-combinations-recombination-part-region.jpg)During meiosis, an event known as chromosomal crossing over sometimes occurs as a part of recombination. In this process, a region of one chromosome is exchanged for a region of another chromosome, thereby producing unique chromosomal combinations that further divide into haploid daughter cells.(more)

Every organism contains a basic set of chromosomes, [unique](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/unique) in number and size for every species, that includes the complete set of genes plus any DNA between them. While the term _genome_ was not brought into use until 1920, the existence of genomes has been known since the late 19th century, when chromosomes were first observed as stained bodies visible under the [microscope](https://www.britannica.com/technology/microscope). The initial discovery of chromosomes was then followed in the 20th century by the mapping of genes on chromosomes based on the frequency of exchange of parts of chromosomes by a process called chromosomal crossing over, an event that occurs as a part of the normal process of [recombination](https://www.britannica.com/science/recombination-genetics) and the production of sex cells ([gametes](https://www.britannica.com/science/gamete)) during [meiosis](https://www.britannica.com/science/meiosis-cytology). The genes that could be mapped by chromosomal crossing over were mainly those for which mutant [phenotypes](https://www.britannica.com/science/phenotype) (visible [manifestations](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/manifestations) of an organism’s genetic composition) had been observed, only a small proportion of the total genes in the genome. The [discipline](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/discipline) of genomics arose when the technology became available to deduce the complete [nucleotide](https://www.britannica.com/science/nucleotide) sequence of genomes, sequences generally in the range of billions of nucleotide pairs.

[Sequencing](https://www.britannica.com/science/sequence-determination-biology) and bioinformatic analysis of genomes
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[![Image 24: DNA extraction; recombinant DNA](https://cdn.britannica.com/14/101714-050-926C6B64/DNA-process-extraction-molecules-cells-tissues-series.jpg?w=300) 1 of 2](https://cdn.britannica.com/14/101714-050-926C6B64/DNA-process-extraction-molecules-cells-tissues-series.jpg)

[DNA extraction; recombinant DNA](https://cdn.britannica.com/14/101714-050-926C6B64/DNA-process-extraction-molecules-cells-tissues-series.jpg)The process of DNA extraction is necessary to isolate molecules of DNA from cells or tissues. A series of steps, including the use of protease enzymes to strip proteins from the DNA, are required for isolating pure DNA that is suitable for use in later procedures, such as cloning or sequencing.(more)

[![Image 25: DNA sequencing](https://cdn.britannica.com/87/22487-050-739115AC/DNA-fragments-genomics-research-cells-vector-replication.jpg?w=300) 2 of 2](https://cdn.britannica.com/87/22487-050-739115AC/DNA-fragments-genomics-research-cells-vector-replication.jpg)

[DNA sequencing](https://cdn.britannica.com/87/22487-050-739115AC/DNA-fragments-genomics-research-cells-vector-replication.jpg)In genomics research, fragments of genomic DNA are inserted into a vector and amplified by replication in bacterial cells. In this way, large amounts of DNA can be cloned and extracted from the bacterial cells. The DNA is then sequenced and further analyzed using bioinformatics techniques.(more)

Genomic sequences are usually determined using automatic sequencing machines. In a typical experiment to determine a genomic sequence, genomic DNA first is extracted from a sample of cells of an organism and then is broken into many random fragments. These fragments are cloned in a DNA vector (carrier) that is capable of carrying large DNA inserts. Because the total amount of DNA that is required for sequencing and additional experimental analysis is several times the total amount of DNA in an organism’s genome, each of the cloned fragments is [amplified](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/amplified) individually by replication inside a living bacterial cell, which reproduces rapidly and in great quantity to generate many bacterial clones. The cloned DNA is then extracted from the bacterial clones and is fed into the sequencing machine. The resulting sequence data are stored in a computer. When a large enough number of sequences from many different clones is obtained, the computer ties them together using sequence overlaps. The result is the genomic sequence, which is then deposited in a publicly accessible database. (For more information about DNA cloning and sequencing, _see_ the article [recombinant DNA technology](https://www.britannica.com/science/recombinant-DNA-technology).)

A complete genomic sequence in itself is of limited use; the data must be processed to find the genes and, if possible, their associated regulatory sequences. The need for these detailed analyses has given rise to the field of [bioinformatics](https://www.britannica.com/science/bioinformatics), in which computer programs scan DNA sequences looking for genes, using [algorithms](https://www.britannica.com/science/algorithm) based on the known features of genes, such as unique triplet sequences of nucleotides known as start and stop codons that span a gene-sized segment of DNA or sequences of DNA that are known to be important in regulating [adjacent](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/adjacent) genes. Once candidate genes are identified, they must be [annotated](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/annotated) to ascribe potential functions. Such [annotation](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/annotation) is generally based on known functions of similar [gene](https://www.britannica.com/science/gene) sequences in other organisms, a type of analysis made possible by evolutionary conservation of gene sequence and function across organisms as a result of their common ancestry. However, after annotation there is still a subset of genes for which functions cannot be deduced; these functions gradually become revealed with further research.

Genomics applications
---------------------

[Functional genomics](https://www.britannica.com/science/functional-genomics)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

[![Image 26: Know how genomic study of the Australian sheep blowfly can help researchers devise protection against this pest](https://cdn.britannica.com/49/186249-138-106898C6/genomics-insect-pests-control-production-sheep-blow.jpg?w=800&h=450&c=crop)](https://www.britannica.com/video/genomics-insect-pests-control-production-sheep-blow/-204938)

Know how genomic study of the Australian sheep blowfly can help researchers devise protection against this pest Learn how genomics can be applied to the control of insect pests, as in the production of a draft genome of the Australian sheep blow fly (_Lucilia cuprina_).(more)

[See all videos for this article](https://www.britannica.com/science/genomics/images-videos)

Analysis of genes at the functional level is one of the main uses of genomics, an area known generally as functional genomics. Determining the function of individual genes can be done in several ways. Classical, or forward, genetic [methodology](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/methodology) starts with a randomly obtained mutant of interesting phenotype and uses this to find the normal gene sequence and its function. Reverse genetics starts with the normal gene sequence (as obtained by genomics), induces a targeted [mutation](https://www.britannica.com/science/mutation-genetics) into the gene, then, by observing how the mutation changes phenotype, deduces the normal function of the gene. The two approaches, forward and reverse, are complementary. Often a gene identified by forward genetics has been mapped to one specific chromosomal region, and the full genomic sequence reveals a gene in this position with an already annotated function. (For more information about genetic studies, _see_[genetics:](https://www.britannica.com/science/genetics/DNA-and-the-genetic-code#ref48731)[Methods in genetics](https://www.britannica.com/science/genetics/Methods-in-genetics#ref48739).)

Gene identification by [microarray](https://www.britannica.com/technology/microarray) genomic analysis
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[![Image 27: Microarray](https://cdn.britannica.com/40/262340-050-9FBD8959/Microarray-DNA-slide-genetics.jpg?w=300)](https://cdn.britannica.com/40/262340-050-9FBD8959/Microarray-DNA-slide-genetics.jpg)

[Microarray](https://cdn.britannica.com/40/262340-050-9FBD8959/Microarray-DNA-slide-genetics.jpg)A researcher observes fluorescent samples of DNA following microarray analysis.(more)

Genomics has greatly simplified the process of finding the complete subset of genes that is relevant to some specific [temporal](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/temporal) or developmental event of an organism. For example, [microarray](https://www.britannica.com/technology/microarray) technology allows a sample of the DNA of a clone of each gene in a whole genome to be laid out in order on the surface of a special chip, which is basically a small thin piece of glass that is treated in such a way that DNA molecules firmly stick to the surface. For any specific developmental stage of interest (e.g., the growth of root hairs in a plant or the production of a limb bud in an animal), the total RNA is extracted from cells of the organism, labeled with a fluorescent dye, and used to bathe the surfaces of the microarrays. As a result of specific base pairing, the RNAs present bind to the genes from which they were originally [transcribed](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/transcribed) and produce fluorescent spots on the chip’s surface. enriched_points.expanded_content: Omics refers to comprehensive studies of biological molecules, analyzing entire sets such as genomes, proteins, or metabolites, facilitating insights into biological functions and relationships.
Genomics focuses on the structure, function, and mapping of genomes, providing insights into genetic variations and their roles in traits and diseases.
Transcriptomics studies all RNA transcripts within a cell, revealing gene expression patterns and regulatory mechanisms crucial for understanding cellular functions and responses.
Proteomics involves the large-scale analysis of proteins, including their structures and functions, helping to elucidate biological processes and disease mechanisms.
Metabolomics examines the complete set of metabolites in a biological sample, providing insights into metabolic pathways and their influence on health and disease states.
Epigenomics studies chemical modifications on DNA and histones that regulate gene expression, influencing traits without altering the underlying genetic code.
Omics technologies enable the identification of genetic, transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolomic variations linked to specific traits, enhancing breeding and precision medicine strategies.
